title: Ancient Snake Had Legs and a Lost Cheekbone!
summary:
Did you know that ancient snakes had legs? A fossil from Argentina shows a snake that lived 100 million years ago with hind legs! It's called Najash rionegrina.

Scientists used special CT scans to look inside the fossil without breaking it. They found something surprising: this snake had a cheekbone, called the jugal bone. Modern snakes don't have this bone. The discovery corrects a 160-year-old mistake about snake skulls.

The fossil also shows that early snakes were big-bodied and big-mouthed. Scientists used to think snakes started as small animals that burrowed underground. But this fossil proves they were larger and had wide mouths.

Najash still had hind legs, which means snakes kept their legs for a long time before losing them completely. This fossil is like a snapshot of evolution in action—a snake that was still changing from a lizard-like ancestor into the legless snakes we know today.
why_it_matters: This helps us understand that animals change over time, just like how your understanding of the world changes as you grow up.

keywords.explanation: The preserved remains or traces of a plant or animal from long ago.
Limbs that animals use to walk or stand on; ancient snakes had them.
A long, legless reptile that slides on its belly.
A bone in the face below the eye, also called the jugal bone.
Special X-rays that let scientists see inside a fossil without breaking it.
The slow change of living things over millions of years into different forms.

questions.question: What is the name of the ancient snake fossil found in Argentina?
What surprising bone did scientists find in the fossil?
How did scientists look inside the fossil without breaking it?
options: They melted the rock around it
They x-rayed it with a dental machine

questions.question: What did scientists used to think about early snakes?
options: They were large and had wide mouths
They started as small animals that burrowed underground
questions.correct_answer: They started as small animals that burrowed underground

questions.question: What does the fossil prove about when snakes lost their legs?
options: They lost their legs very quickly
They kept their legs for a long time before losing them completely
They lost legs before they developed skulls
questions.correct_answer: They kept their legs for a long time before losing them completely

questions.question: The discovery corrects a mistake that was how many years old?

background_read: Snakes evolved from four-legged lizard-like ancestors over millions of years.
Argentina (a country in South America) has many amazing fossils, including this snake that lived 100 million years ago.
CT scans are like super-detailed X-rays that let scientists see inside rocks and fossils without damaging them.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists using CT scans to study a snake fossil
WHAT: Discovered that an ancient snake called Najash rionegrina had hind legs and a cheekbone that modern snakes lack
WHERE: In Argentina, from a fossil that is 100 million years old
WHY: The finding corrects a 160-year-old mistake about snake skulls and shows that snakes evolved from larger, big-mouthed animals, not small burrowers

perspectives.description: Paleontologists are excited because the fossil fills a gap in snake evolution and shows how early snakes looked very different from today.
Scientists had to rethink their old idea that snakes started as tiny burrowers, which shows that new discoveries can change what we thought we knew.
This fossil is like a picture of evolution in action, showing a snake that was still partway between lizard and modern snake.
